words: ROB MOORE: Were there other people who had other kinds of carts or was there
just this popcorn guy?
DELLA BEARDSLEY J0HNS0N: No, it was just this popcorn man. He had been using
my butter for quite a while, getting it at the store. So he told me he
said, "You just let me have all the butter you can make." He says, "You
don't even have to roll it in those little pound rolls" ha says, "you
just pack it in crocks or anything like that." That's the way he wanted
it. So I sold it to him until we got so we sold cream.

speaker: ROB
words: How much would you sell him? How much butter did you produce?

speaker: DJB
words: Well, I'd sell him about 25 pounds a week.

speaker: ROB
words: Did he pop the popcorn in the butter too, in addition to spreading it

speaker: ROB
words: on top?

speaker: DJB
words: Um-huh.

speaker: ROB
words: How long was he around there, how long did he do this?

speaker: DJB
words: Oh, quite a while. 'Course I don't remember the dates and everything now.

speaker: DJB
words: My memory isn't as good as it used to be. Anyway that's a long time ago.

speaker: ROB
words: What was the business district of Moscow like in the early days?

speaker: DJB
words: Oh, in the early days...I don't know. I know we were married in '13

speaker: DJB
words: and we had to have four horses on a wagon to go in and get our little dab

speaker: DJB
words: of furniture that we bought there at Williamson's. And oneof the horses

speaker: DJB
words: was just a colt and it wasn't used to city life I guess, such as it was.

speaker: DJB
words: So this Williamson wanted my husband to drive up Main Street, and he (my

speaker: DJB
words: husband) wanted to take a back street and come in the back part of the

speaker: DJB
words: store. He wanted him to come up main street because he thought that would

speaker: DJB
words: advertise his...

speaker: ROB
words: Ohhh.

speaker: DJB
words: ...his store. And the colt got frightened at something and they had a

speaker: DJB
words: runaway right there on Main Street and got one horse down and dragged him

speaker: DJB
words: a ways. And finally with the policeman and everybody and they got 'em

speaker: DJB
words: stopped. But it took four horses on the wagon. We didn't have a very big

speaker: DJB
words: load either, course we were just starting out and doing with as little as

speaker: DJB
words: we could. And they finally got loaded up, and started out home. Why I'll

speaker: DJB
words: tell you the roads were something terrible. Right there on Main Street it

speaker: DJB
words: was just nothing but mud.

speaker: ROB
words: Ohhh. It took four horses because the wagon sank so deep?

speaker: DJB
words: Um-hm. We didn't have all these highways and blacktops and everything

speaker: DJB
words: like they have nowadays. Well, that was in '13. So...

speaker: ROB
words: Around that time were there a lot of stores in Moscow?

speaker: DJB
words: Not too many. No.

speaker: ROB
words: There would just be one store of each type, like one hardware, and one

speaker: ROB
words: furniture or...

speaker: DJB
words: Mostly that. One hospital. It was kind of a one-sided town. (Chuckles)

speaker: ROB
words: How often did you go into Moscow from the farm?

speaker: DJB
words: Well, in those days we had to travel with horses so we didn't go very

speaker: DJB
words: often, not any oftener than we had to. Oh it was quite a while. It must

speaker: DJB
words: have been four years before we finally got our first Ford.

speaker: ROB
words: I bet that was really an experience.

speaker: DJB
words: Yeh, it really was. I never will forget it because my husband, he took

speaker: DJB
words: the wheel. He thought he knew just how to drive it and everything. And

speaker: DJB
words: he picked out the crookedest road in the neighborhood. (Laughs) And I'll

speaker: DJB
words: tell you I was hanging on the back there. I was in the back seat, and I

speaker: DJB
words: was hanging on with both hands. He'd go around those turns and he'd never

speaker: DJB
words: slow down or anything. I just wondered when we'd go over.

speaker: ROB
words: Do you think he knew where the brake was?

speaker: DJB
words: Oh, I guess he did know where the brake was. But I don't know. It was

speaker: DJB
words: a wild ride believe me.

speaker: ROB
words: There's an old story about a guy who got his first car. An old Ford.

speaker: ROB
words: He was driving along and came to a corner and wanted to stop and didn't

speaker: ROB
words: know how. And he gripped onto the wheel and he stomped both feet down and

speaker: ROB
words: he said "Whoa." And when he stomped his feet down he hit the gas pedal.

speaker: DJB
words: Well, that's the way I did with our Chevrolet. We finally got rid of

speaker: DJB
words: the Ford, and we got a Chevrolet. And I kept after my husband to show me

speaker: DJB
words: how to run it, 'cause I thought...well it's quite necessary, I think,

speaker: DJB
words: especially when you're out in the country that way. You might need to know

speaker: DJB
words: how to run it just real sudden sometime. And he said,

speaker: DJB
words: "well no" he says " No I don't think I'll show you how to run this

speaker: DJB
words: because" he says "I don't intend to keep it. I don't like it and I don't

speaker: DJB
words: think I'll keep it very long." And so my brother-in-law was visiting me

speaker: DJB
words: and he said,

speaker: DJB
words: "well" he says "Delia you should learn to run that car." And well, I

speaker: DJB
words: told him what Harry had said, that he was going to trade it in. "Well"

speaker: DJB
words: he says "I think you should learn to run it because then you can run the

speaker: DJB
words: next car, too."

speaker: DJB
words: So I started out from home and I had a terrible headache that day and

speaker: DJB
words: I shouldn't even taken ahold of a wheel, but he was going to leave the next

speaker: DJB
words: day. So he wanted me to be sure and run that car before he left. And we

speaker: DJB
words: got started down the road and then we had to make a kind of a...Oh it was

speaker: DJB
words: quite a little steep hill. And there was some cows laying down, you know

speaker: DJB
words: how they'll lay down right in the middle of the road where it's soft.

speaker: DJB
words: (Laughs) And they were laying down right there in the middle of the road.

speaker: DJB
words: Well, I didn't know how to stop it or anything. So I jumped the bank, went

speaker: DJB
words: up into the stubble field. It was after harvest. I went up into the stubble

speaker: DJB
words: field. I took out two posts (laugh) and tore one of the doors off, the back

speaker: DJB
words: door. But that's the only mark that was on the car was those screws just

speaker: DJB
words: pulled out. And so he helped me get it back down in the road. Didn't tip

speaker: DJB
words: over, it was right side up still. Got down in the road, I drove it down to

speaker: DJB
words: the mailbox, that was about a mile away. And drove it back in and everything.

speaker: DJB
words: I was so mad at that car I think I could have driven it anyway. (Lauohs)

speaker: DJB
words: And my sister she said,

speaker: DJB
words: "Well what in the world did you let her have that accident like that,"

speaker: DJB
words: she says "it'll bother her now about driving all the time." Well he says,

speaker: DJB
words: "She did it so quickly," he says "I didn't know what to do."

speaker: DJB
words: So I asked my husband if I could run it around in the flat. We had a

speaker: DJB
words: big flat pasture there. And I said,

speaker: DJB
words: "I can run it around in there and if I don't know how to stop it I'll

speaker: DJB
words: just run it until the oil runs out." (Laughs)

speaker: DJB
words: And "No," he says "you mustn't take it out, you mustn't take it out

speaker: DJB
words: unless I'm here." And so I told my mother, she was living with me then,

speaker: DJB
words: I said,

speaker: DJB
words: "I've a notion to take that car out anyway because I know I can drive

speaker: DJB
words: it now." It had a real good book of directions with it and I had studied

speaker: DJB
words: those so thoroughly. I says, "I know I'll never make that mistake again."

speaker: DJB
words: So she says,

speaker: DJB
words: "Well, as long as he told you not to, I don't think I would."

speaker: DJB
words: And then later he did trade it in for a Chevrolet. He came home one

speaker: DJB
words: day and he was in an awful hurry to go to town. Broke down something and

speaker: DJB
words: he wanted to get some repairs and wanted 'em in a hurry. And he went down

speaker: DJB
words: and he worked, and he worked and he worked in the garage with that car and

speaker: DJB
words: he couldn't get it out to save his life. My mother was watching him out

speaker: DJB
words: the window and She said,

speaker: DJB
words: "Well, he evidently can't get that car started, there's something wrong."

speaker: DJB
words: Well, he came up finally to the house, he says,

speaker: DJB
words: "Do you suppose you can get that car out of the garage?"

speaker: ROB
words: He knew you'd been studying, huh?

speaker: DJB
words: Uh-uh. And I says, "Yes, I know I can," just as confident as I could be.

speaker: DJB
words: And he says, "well I wish you* would then." So I went down and got it out

speaker: DJB
words: just as slick as could be, and then I turned the wheel over to him# That

speaker: DJB
words: was the last of my experience with a car. I never learned to drive.

speaker: ROB
words: Really? It sounds like you have a natural talent for it, though.

speaker: DJB
words: Well, I don't know I never will forget that experience because he was

speaker: DJB
words: so confident that he could run that car out. And he found out that he

speaker: DJB
words: couldn't, so he had to call on me then to get the car out of the garage s

speaker: DJB
words: (laughs) so he could go to town. But I never drove the car again or any

speaker: DJB
words: (car). Course we had different cars after that. But that was my last

speaker: DJB
words: experience. But when he finally said that he was going to trade in that

speaker: DJB
words: Ford and get something else, why I thought he never had noticed that I'd

speaker: DJB
words: had an accident with it or anything. And so I thought it over quite a bit,

speaker: DJB
words: and I thought well maybe I should tell him before he trades it in because

speaker: DJB
words: maybe a mechanic or somebody...

speaker: ROB
words: You put the door back on?

speaker: DJB
words: Yea, we got some longer screws and put the door back on. Then there was

speaker: DJB
words: just a few scratches down inside the whatchacallit, the fender, and that was

speaker: DJB
words: the only thing that showed about it. And so I told him about it and he looked

speaker: DJB
words: at it. Well, he says, "I never would have found that, I never would have

speaker: DJB
words: known." If I hadn't had told him he never would have known that I had an

speaker: DJB
words: accident with the car. What I should have done was gone ahead then, after I had that

speaker: DJB
words: accident

speaker: DJB
words: and after studying those directions thoroughly, was to just gone ahead and

speaker: DJB
words: driven.

speaker: ROB
words: Uh-hm.

speaker: DJB
words: Because I was so confident then that I could do it. But then he kept

speaker: DJB
words: changing cars and getting different cars. So I gave up the idea.

speaker: ROB
words: Well, before you had the cars you'd gained a lot of experience with horses

speaker: ROB
words: hadn't you?

speaker: DJB
words: Oh yes. Yes, I lived on the back of a horse from the time I was six

speaker: DJB
words: years old, til we came up here.

speaker: ROB
words: You spent quite a bit of time on the back of a horse coming up here too,

speaker: ROB
words: didn't you?

speaker: DJB
words: Oh yes. Yes, we drove loose horses all the way from California up here.

speaker: ROB
words: Where in California?

speaker: DJB
words: Oh up in the northeast corner, Modell County. We came through Lakeview,

speaker: DJB
words: Baker...

speaker: ROB
words: What kind of trip was that? How did that trip go?

speaker: DJB
words: Well, it was a rough trip. I wouldn't want to go through it again.

speaker: DJB
words: Because it was really a pioneer trip. It was awful hard on my mother. Her

speaker: DJB
words: brother was going to come up with her and then he backed out the last minute.

speaker: DJB
words: Well, then she had to hurry up and get some man. The man that was recommended

speaker: DJB
words: to her drank, but they said that he had given his word that he wouldn't

speaker: DJB
words: drink any on the way. He was a good driver, good with horses, and all that,

speaker: DJB
words: so we made it. After we got to Moscow, why he sure tanked up quite a bit

speaker: DJB
words: (laughs).

speaker: ROB
words: What kind of things happened along the trip? Did you always manage to

speaker: ROB
words: keep the horses under control or did you ever have trouble with them or

speaker: ROB
words: anything?

speaker: DJB
words: Oh no we had pretty good luck with them. But they had never been on a

speaker: DJB
words: ferry boat before and we had to ferry two rivers. And this one down here

speaker: DJB
words: between Anatone...you know down in there.

speaker: ROB
words: The Grand Ronde?

speaker: DJB
words: Grand Ronde. And there they broke back. They made us get off of our

speaker: DJB
words: horses and just hold the reins and not be mounted on 'cause it was too

speaker: DJB
words: dangerous at that time. So they got excited, the loose horses got excited

speaker: DJB
words: and the first thing they knew they broke the barrier down and got back on

speaker: DJB
words: the land. And then we had to chase *em so far before we could get around

speaker: DJB
words: 'em because...I don't know whether you know the way those highways are now,

speaker: DJB
words: but course they're a lot better now than they were then. But it was right

speaker: DJB
words: along the river, just a steep sidehill up above and the river down below.

speaker: DJB
words: And when we got back my mother was just in hysterics. She just was sure

speaker: DJB
words: those horses had pushed us off of the grade and into the river. She just

speaker: DJB
words: couldn't get it out of her head that anything else could possibly happen.

speaker: ROB
words: That was you and your sister who went chasing after them?

speaker: DJB
words: Um-huh. Yea.

speaker: ROB
words: How old where you on this trip?

speaker: DJB
words: Well, I was sixteen and she was twelve.

speaker: ROB
words: How many horses were you driving?

speaker: DJB
words: Well, we had four horses on the wagon, two horses on the hack and then we

speaker: DJB
words: had our saddle horses. And then we had, I don't know, 6 or 8 loose horses.

speaker: ROB
words: Were they all riding horses or were some of the loose horses...

speaker: DJB
words: No, some of them were work horses. And coming up that grade out of the

speaker: DJB
words: Grand Ronde River. They kept horses down there just to help immigrants up

speaker: DJB
words: the grade. And so mother inquired about the price and she says

speaker: DJB
words: "Well I just can't afford that." And he says,

speaker: DJB
words: "Well you'll never make it with the horses that you have."

speaker: DJB
words: "Well," she says, "We'll have to try it. Because" she says, "I can't

speaker: DJB
words: afford your prices." So we started up the hill and we wore out every horse

speaker: DJB
words: we had on that grade coming up out of the Grand Ronde. And then that night

speaker: DJB
words: it rained, oh it rained something terrible! There was a couple of friends

speaker: DJB
words: that we knew real well. Man and his son that had...I've forgotten just

speaker: DJB
words: where we'd picked them up. But anyway, they traveled with us for quite a

speaker: DJB
words: ways. And then when we got there at the Grand Ronde River why they decided

speaker: DJB
words: they would go one way, take one road and we'd go another. (Heppner) I think

speaker: DJB
words: was the name of the road where they wanted to go through (Heppner). And

speaker: DJB
words: that night they camped just out of (Heppner) and they had that awful big

speaker: DJB
words: flood there. So many people were drowned. They went in in the morning

speaker: DJB
words: and helped take out a lot of bodies that were drowned in the (Heppner)

speaker: DJB
words: flood. So we thought we were lucky that we didn't go with them. Because

speaker: DJB
words: we could have gone around that way but we just thought if we could get up

speaker: DJB
words: that hill it would make it shorter to Moscow. So we finally made it but

speaker: DJB
words: every horse we had was just completely worn out.

speaker: ROB
words: Did you make it all the way to the top in one day?

speaker: DJB
words: Yeh, we made it. But then when we got here to Lewiston why there was a

speaker: DJB
words: toll bridge^across the Clearwater, and I think there was a ferry that crossed

speaker: DJB
words: the Clearwater up above too. But we decided, with the experience that we

speaker: DJB
words: had on the ferries and all that, maybe we'd better try the toll bridge. And

speaker: DJB
words: that fellow he just took advantage of my mother. He just charged her an awful

speaker: DJB
words: price because he thought she was a woman and an immigrant. So we paid an

speaker: DJB
words: awful toll to get across there but we got across and then we got here to

speaker: DJB
words: Lewiston we had to ferry again to get across the Clearwater. There was

speaker: DJB
words: no bridges.

speaker: ROB
words: Across the Snake you mean.

speaker: DJB
words: No the Clearwater.

speaker: ROB
words: The toll bridge was across the Snake?

speaker: DJB
words: Yea.

speaker: ROB
words: Oh I see.

speaker: DJB
words: So we camped over there where the roundup grounds are that night. Then

speaker: DJB
words: it took us all the next day to get up the grade and get to Moscow. We

speaker: DJB
words: had a little better luck crossing the Clearwater. But I know

speaker: DJB
words: we had quite a time my sister and I. When we would go through a town or

speaker: DJB
words: knew we were going through a town of any size, why we would both ride and

speaker: DJB
words: drive the horses, keep the horses together and drive them across. We knew

speaker: DJB
words: we were going through Lewiston. Why, that was supposed to be a pretty big

speaker: DJB
words: town then. So we both rode and we had quite a time keeping them off of

speaker: DJB
words: the lawns everywhere. They were always where they shouldn't be. But we

speaker: DJB
words: finally made it. Then when we got up here, it took us so long on the road

speaker: DJB
words: and everything that when we got up here the market had gone out of horses

speaker: DJB
words: and we just almost had to give them away. So we would have been ahead I

speaker: DJB
words: guess to just have gotten rid of them there. But there was no market for

speaker: DJB
words: them there in California. Everybody had their own horses, raised their

speaker: DJB
words: own horses. Well, I don't know. It was a terrible trip. I wouldn't

speaker: DJB
words: want to go through it again.

speaker: ROB
words: How did you eat and where did you sleep on the trip?

speaker: DJB
words: Well, we had a tent and we put that up every night. I don't know how

speaker: DJB
words: we would have stood that awful rain we had at Anatone if it hadn't been for

speaker: DJB
words: that tent. That tent protected us and we had a cupboard built in the back

speaker: DJB
words: end of the big wagon. And all of our dishes and supplies and everything in

speaker: DJB
words: that. Then it had a trap door that let down and a leg on it and so that

speaker: DJB
words: served as our table. And mother had a little sheet iron stove and my, she

speaker: DJB
words: used to bake the best biscuits and everything on that little stove. It

speaker: DJB
words: might have been just because we were out in the open and traveling that it

speaker: DJB
words: tasted so good but it did taste so good.

speaker: ROB
words: When you look back on it now, you say it was a terrible trip. When you

speaker: ROB
words: were doing it did you kind of enjoy being out and moving along like that?

speaker: DJB
words: Well, I think we girls probably enjoyed it more than mother did. Mother

speaker: DJB
words: worried so much about everything and it was a hard trip on her.

speaker: ROB
words: Well, she had the responsibilty.

speaker: DJB
words: Yea. But I think if her brother had come with us like he planned on

speaker: DJB
words: doing, that it would have made quite a bit of difference. But having this

speaker: DJB
words: other man with us, and as I say when we crossed the ferry here on the Clear

speaker: DJB
words: water why mother told the ferryman what they had charged her to cross the

speaker: DJB
words: toll bridge. And oh he was just furious. He says, "That's just awful."

speaker: DJB
words: And so he wanted her to go back with him. He'd take her back over there

speaker: DJB
words: and make that toll bridge man divvy up because he had overcharged her.

speaker: DJB
words: But "oh" she says, "I'm so tired, I'm so near the end of my trip" she says

speaker: DJB
words: "I just don't feel like I could put up with any more." So she didn't go

speaker: DJB
words: back and argue with him. But he was just furious about it.

speaker: ROB
words: How long did the trip take you?

speaker: DJB
words: It took us a month.

speaker: ROB
words: When you arrived in Moscow where did you settle, where did you have to go?

speaker: DJB
words: Well, after we sold the horses, we sold them here and there and every

speaker: DJB
words: where, and of course so many of the farmers up around Moscow turned their

speaker: DJB
words: horses out to strawstacks in the wintertime. And our horses were all used

speaker: DJB
words: to nice timothy hay. So I guess some of them they couldn't get used to that.

speaker: DJB
words: We went to an uncle's there. Mother's sister and mother's mother lived there.

speaker: DJB
words: Then she had a brother that lived in Moscow. But we went to this sister's

speaker: DJB
words: until we disposed of the horses and then we bought a little home up on

speaker: DJB
words: Spotswood Street. So we lived there then until my sister and I were married

speaker: DJB
words: there.

speaker: ROB
words: At that house?

speaker: DJB
words: Uh-hm.

speaker: ROB
words: What did your mother do to support her family?

speaker: DJB
words: Well, she was a practical nurse. So she did nursing, but she was sick

speaker: DJB
words: an awful lot. She had gallstone trouble and she was sick an awful lot.

speaker: DJB
words: And the doctors, they made a awful mistake in her case because they said

speaker: DJB
words: they operated on her and they found that the gallbladder was just obliterated

speaker: DJB
words: and couldn't even find gallstones or anything. But we found out later on

speaker: DJB
words: that that was all a mistake. She finally died of gallstones.

speaker: ROB
words: Wasn't it a pretty hard life on a woman alone in a pioneer type town?

speaker: DJB
words: Oh yea. That's one reason why we left there. The neighbors were all so

speaker: DJB
words: good. They rounded up the cattle, and of course cattle were a fair price...

speaker: ROB
words: Why you left California?

speaker: DJB
words: Um-huh. So we sold those. The neighbors were real good to help her and

speaker: DJB
words: everything, but still they have their own work to do and you can't expect

speaker: DJB
words: them...course they were quite a ways apart too. It was kind of hard for us.

speaker: ROB
words: Was it more pioneering"in California than it was here? Was it a further

speaker: ROB
words: isolated kind of place?

speaker: DJB
words: Well, yes it was pioneering there too. We didn't have telephones, and

speaker: DJB
words: we didn't have.our nearest doctor was 37 miles. The only way you could

speaker: DJB
words: go back and forth was horseback. We didn't even have roads or anything.

speaker: DJB
words: Only just mere trails so...

speaker: ROB
words: Why did your mother decide to move to Idaho? Why did she pick Idaho?

speaker: DJB
words: Well, because her folks were up here. So many of her folks. She had

speaker: DJB
words: a brother down near 3uliaetta on Fix Ridge. And she had a brother in

speaker: DJB
words: Spokane, and then she had this brother in Moscow, and a sister and her

speaker: DJB
words: mother were in Moscow. So her family were practically out here, all that

speaker: DJB
words: had come west.

speaker: ROB
words: Had they gone to California and then come back, or settled in here to

speaker: ROB
words: begin with?

speaker: DJB
words: Well, some of them. My brother that was in Moscow was in California

speaker: DJB
words: awhile then he came back here. Settled here. So that's a long story and

speaker: DJB
words: it's sure a pioneer story. (Laughs)

speaker: ROB
words: What kind of difficulties do you think your mother had that a woman with

speaker: ROB
words: a husband wouldn't have in a pioneer society, in the kind of society that

speaker: ROB
words: Moscow was in the early days? What kind of things did she have to do extra?

speaker: DJB
words: Well, I don't know, really, what you mean.

speaker: ROB
words: Well, I was just thinking I can kinda see how it would be very hard for

speaker: ROB
words: a woman alone, raising a family, in a pioneer situation. And I wonder if

speaker: ROB
words: you can think of any examples of things she would have to do. Do you think

speaker: ROB
words: she would have to work a lot harder than most people?

speaker: DJB
words: Oh, yes. My mother always had to work hard. She didn't have very much

speaker: DJB
words: to do with. That's why we tried to bring the horses up here. She thought

speaker: DJB
words: she could get more for them. And of course my father I don't know how much

speaker: DJB
words: of this I should tell. He just left home. And left her with two girls.

speaker: DJB
words: And we didn't even know he was thinking about going or anything. He just

speaker: DJB
words: got on his horse and rode off one day and we never saw him again. Well,

speaker: DJB
words: there was a red-headed schoolmarm mixed up with it. But mother worked

speaker: DJB
words: awfully hard. She did nursing when she was well enough so she could. Why

speaker: DJB
words: and then managing everything the best she could. She was bound and determined

speaker: DJB
words: she wasn't going to spend anything that was left to her. Her share of the

speaker: DJB
words: property and everything. So she did work awfully hard. She was a wonderful

speaker: DJB
words: mother.

speaker: ROB
words: Did you girls work too, to try and help out?

speaker: DJB
words: Oh yes we did, and I went to school. I went to the University almost four

speaker: DJB
words: years. Then the University burned down just before the last term that I

speaker: DJB
words: went. So, I thought I'd go back when they got things straightened out again,

speaker: DJB
words: I'd go back and finish up. But I started working for a dressmaker in

speaker: DJB
words: Moscow and she taught me sewing. She took students. Anybody that she could

speaker: DJB
words: get along with. So many of them said, "How in the world do you do it? We

speaker: DJB
words: couldn't get along with her at all." And I said, "Well I don't know." We

speaker: DJB
words: always got along just fine. I got started in the dressmaking that way and

speaker: DJB
words: I graduated from the course. And then I got to sewing on my own, because

speaker: DJB
words: mother was sick so much. I could be at home with her and still I could

speaker: DJB
words: carry on this sewing. But I got this sewing to going so strenuous that it

speaker: DJB
words: was I think...that was one thing my husband always said, "Well, I rescued

speaker: DJB
words: you just in time." (Laughs) My sister worked in the laundry up there too

speaker: DJB
words: quite a bit, during vacations. But I thought well afterwards I got to

speaker: DJB
words: thinking about it and I thought well, maybe it's more necessary to keep her

speaker: DJB
words: in school then to go back and try to finish up myself. And I'm glad that

speaker: DJB
words: I did because I think it was the best way.

speaker: ROB
words: What was this dressmaking woman that taught you dressmaking, what was her

speaker: ROB
words: name?

speaker: DJB
words: Well, it just slips my mind right now.

speaker: ROB
words: Okay, we'll come back to it. The other question I had was...when the

speaker: ROB
words: University burned did they just close down the school completely?

speaker: DJB
words: Oh, no. They had kind of divided us up. So that was another thing that

speaker: DJB
words: was awfully hard on me too. I'd have one subject up at the...well, they

speaker: DJB
words: sent part of them to the gymnasiam that was just a new building then. So

speaker: DJB
words: I'd have to go there one period, and then to the Carnegie Library and that

speaker: DJB
words: was clear across town and I just couldn't make it. And of course we lived

speaker: DJB
words: on Spotswood Street and that was clear across town. So I just decided that

speaker: DJB
words: I just couldn't make it. So I thought well, it's better to keep my sister

speaker: DJB
words: in school than it was to keep me. Mother was having an awful struggle to

speaker: DJB
words: keep us in school anyway so...

speaker: ROB
words: How old were you when you married?

speaker: DJB
words: I was 26 when I got married.

speaker: ROB
words: As soon as you got married you moved out to the farm outside of 3ohnson?

speaker: DJB
words: Well, we lived on Harry's sister's farm at first. That's six miles from

speaker: DJB
words: Moscow. And then later we started paying for our own farm. And that made

speaker: DJB
words: us a little farther. We were really closer to Pullman but still farther

speaker: DJB
words: from Moscow.

speaker: ROB
words: What was life like on the farm in the early days?

speaker: DJB
words: Well, we sure didn't have the conveniences that you have nowadays. We

speaker: DJB
words: didn't have electric lights, we didn't have telephones or automobiles or

speaker: DJB
words: anything like that. We farmed with horses, we started in with horses and

speaker: DJB
words: so it was quite different than it is nowadays. Nowadays the farmers have

speaker: DJB
words: so many conveniences. I know that our farm has been improved. My nephew

speaker: DJB
words: is living on it and he has more time for other things.

speaker: ROB
words: What was the farmwife's day like? What kind of things would she have to

speaker: ROB
words: do to keep her house running? I have a pretty good idea of what the farmers

speaker: ROB
words: did, but I don't know much about what the farmwives did.

speaker: DJB
words: Oh well, we always had lots of cooking to do. Because we had to have

speaker: DJB
words: lots more hired help then than they do now with tinis modern machinery. And

speaker: DJB
words: I worked outside an awful lot. I planted all kinds of berry bushes, gathered

speaker: DJB
words: the fruit, sent it to town,and...

speaker: ROB
words: What time would you get up in the morning to start cooking breakfast for

speaker: ROB
words: all the hands and your husband and the kids?

speaker: DJB
words: Oh, I'd have to get up about 5:30 or around there.

speaker: ROB
words: How many meals a day did you have to cook for everybody?

speaker: DJB
words: Well, three. And then sometimes luncheons if they were working in the

speaker: DJB
words: field away from home. We rented quite a bit of land too. So sometimes

speaker: DJB
words: they'd be quite a little ways from home.

speaker: ROB
words: Was everybody usually a pretty big eater?

speaker: DJB
words: Oh my, you couldn't fill them up. (Laughs) Especially in haying time

speaker: DJB
words: when there'd be so many young fellas, you know. My it seemed like you just

speaker: DJB
words: couldn't hardly fill them up. I know one time my husband's father told my

speaker: DJB
words: mother he said "I was just a little bit dubious of Harry marrying a city

speaker: DJB
words: girl." But he says "she's alright." (Laughs)

speaker: ROB
words: 'Cause you could keep up with their eating there?

speaker: DJB
words: Well, I could keep up with the eating. And then I raised most of the

speaker: DJB
words: garden. I did most of the garden work and I had a lot of chickens. That

speaker: DJB
words: was quite a chore. Gathering up eggs, they'd be laying everywhere. So you

speaker: DJB
words: can always find something to do. I was always the first one up in the

speaker: DJB
words: morning, and the last one to bed at night. But those were good old days.

speaker: DJB
words: It's very different from what they have nowadays but still I think those

speaker: DJB
words: was the happiest days we had.

speaker: ROB
words: What kind of things would you do in the odd moments where you might have

speaker: ROB
words: a little time to spare, felt like some entertainment or something like

speaker: ROB
words: that. What kind of things would you do?

speaker: DJB
words: Well we never had any odd times. (Laughs) Well, when we did why it

speaker: DJB
words: would be in the evening. We'd go to Grange or something like that.

speaker: ROB
words: What was the Grange like in the early days?

speaker: DJB
words: Well, I don't know it was just a get-together and the neighbors neighbored

speaker: DJB
words: together. It was a lot different than it is nowadays. It just seemed like

speaker: DJB
words: people were thinking about each other. Nowadays they all think for them

speaker: DJB
words: selves.

speaker: ROB
words: In the wintertime, when when your work was a little slower, would you get

speaker: ROB
words: to get around and do quite a bit of visiting and socializing and stuff?

speaker: DJB
words: Oh yes, we'd go out. We'd have people, neighbors at our house for dinner

speaker: DJB
words: on Sundays, and we'd go to their house on Sundays. We'd go to Grange on

speaker: DJB
words: Saturday nights.

speaker: ROB
words: Did the Grange have parties after the meetings?

speaker: DJB
words: Oh yes. They'd have dances and different things. Mostly dances.

speaker: ROB
words: Did you get shivareed when you got married?

speaker: DJB
words: Yes, oh yes.

speaker: ROB
words: What did they do to you?

speaker: DJB
words: (Laughs) Well, for one thing we had gotten a buggy. We had to have some

speaker: DJB
words: way some way that I could go back and forth to town because my husband couldn't

speaker: DJB
words: always go when I did. And so I bought the buggy and (my mother) gave me a

speaker: DJB
words: horse, bought the horse for me, and I used to go back and forth to town.

speaker: DJB
words: After they shivareed us, why we went out and that buggy was up on the fence.

speaker: DJB
words: We wondered quite awhile how we was going to get it down, because this fence

speaker: DJB
words: around the house was flat on top. It had a flat board on the top and it was

speaker: DJB
words: balanced up there. (Laughs)

speaker: ROB
words: What was the idea? How did shivaree get started or what was the idea of

speaker: ROB
words: a shivaree?

speaker: DJB
words: Well, I don't know. I don't know really. They didn't have shivaree on

speaker: DJB
words: us til we'd been married several weeks and so we just kinda forgot about

speaker: DJB
words: it and then they came one night when we weren't looking for it. I guess the

speaker: DJB
words: worse thing they did was put that buggy up where we wondered how we was

speaker: DJB
words: going to get it down.

speaker: ROB
words: Didn't they make a lot of noise too and try to disturb you and everything?

speaker: DJB
words: Yes. They had all kinds of tinware to pound on.

speaker: ROB
words: Did you have them in then, or did you leave them outside until they finally

speaker: ROB
words: went away?

speaker: DJB
words: Well, we had them in. We told them that we had waited so long our treats

speaker: DJB
words: were gone. But we made out some way. They had been to one other place that

speaker: DJB
words: night. I guess they were treated there too. So they didn't care too much

speaker: DJB
words: whether they had any more treats or not. I guess they decided they'd do the

speaker: DJB
words: two at one time. In those days when you had to travel with horse and buggy,

speaker: DJB
words: why it was kind of a slow process anyway.

speaker: ROB
words: Did most everybody get a shivaree when they got married?

speaker: DJB
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: ROB
words: Did that happen in the towns too or just in the country?

speaker: DJB
words: No, I think it happened in towns too before they got too thickly settled.

speaker: ROB
words: When you were making butter, how did you make butter? What kind of things

speaker: ROB
words: did you use to make the butter?

speaker: DJB
words: Well, I had this little churn that you turned by crank. Then my uncle,

speaker: DJB
words: he was cabinet maker and he lived with us quite a bit, he made me a little

speaker: DJB
words: butter worker. It come to the point like this and was wider up here.

speaker: ROB
words: Kind of a wedge-shaped thing?

speaker: DJB
words: Uh-hm. And then the third leg down there was a little shorter so that the

speaker: DJB
words: Jrine would run down and run out through a little hole in there. He made,

speaker: DJB
words: well I don't know what you call them now. Anyway, it was a little lever

speaker: DJB
words: that worked back and forth over the butter. Squeezed the brine out, the

speaker: DJB
words: extra orine.

speaker: ROB
words: Did you make your own soap and stuff like that too?

speaker: DJB
words: Oh yes.

speaker: ROB
words: How is soap made?

speaker: DJB
words: Well, it's kind of complicated to...I don't know.

speaker: ROB
words: Would it take a long time to make a batch of soap?

speaker: DJB
words: It would take quite a little while. It'd take...well after you got it all

speaker: DJB
words: made why then you had to let it cure for several days and then you could cut

speaker: DJB
words: it into bars.

speaker: ROB
words: Would one batch last you quite awhile?

speaker: DJB
words: Oh yes, it would last quite awhile.

speaker: ROB
words: As I understand it, there were two different kinds of developments with

speaker: ROB
words: thrashing crews. They first ate in the farms and after that they had their

speaker: ROB
words: own cook-wagon. Did you ever cook for a thrashing crew. I mean...

speaker: DJB
words: No. But my mother did when she first came here. She went with a cook

speaker: DJB
words: wagon for a I think it was three years. She made her own bread and her own

speaker: DJB
words: pies and her own cookies and everything. And lots of times (we didn't have

speaker: DJB
words: decent roads then or anything going over the hills) why she'd have to hold

speaker: DJB
words: that bread in her hands to keep it from spilling out.

speaker: ROB
words: She had to cook as they traveled in addition to cooking when they were

speaker: ROB
words: stopped?

speaker: DJB
words: Well, no. She wouldn't try to bake anything or anything like that. But

speaker: DJB
words: she would have to hold this bread to keep it from tipping over. But she

speaker: DJB
words: worked awfully hard at it, I'll tell ya.

speaker: ROB
words: Did you cook for any thrashing crews that came to your farm or did they

speaker: ROB
words: all bring cook-wagons?

speaker: DJB
words: No, we always had a combine. Harry's father had a combine. So they would

speaker: DJB
words: harvest for him and then harvest for us too. And then we had I think it

speaker: DJB
words: was 20 head of horses on a combine. So we'd use his father's horses and

speaker: DJB
words: his horses. That way they got their combining done.

speaker: ROB
words: Did you usually keep a lot of stock at one time?

speaker: DJB
words: Well, we kept quite a few and then we had to keep lots of horses cause

speaker: DJB
words: they did all their farming with horses.

speaker: ROB
words: Did you have riding horses also?

speaker: DJB
words: Oh no we didn't. Oh after I got here to this country why we kind of quit

speaker: DJB
words: riding. 'Course there in California where we lived there was no boys in

speaker: DJB
words: the family. So we had to ride a lot there. And bring in stock and different

speaker: DJB
words: things. I never will forget what a hard time we had. My sister and I, we

speaker: DJB
words: were sent out to get some of our horses that were mixed with other horses,

speaker: DJB
words: and if you ever have any experience of that kind you never forget it. Be

speaker: DJB
words: cause it's just almost impossible to separate them, you know. We finally

speaker: DJB
words: did, we finally got them separated and got our own home.

speaker: ROB
words: Would you have to rope them out or just try to cut them out?

speaker: DJB
words: just cut them out. Sometimes you didn't know whether you were going to

speaker: DJB
words: stay on your horse or not. The horses would know which way they were going

speaker: DJB
words: to turn, but you wouldn't.

speaker: ROB
words: Huh.

speaker: DJB
words: So we used to do lots of riding. I had a little brother but he died be

speaker: DJB
words: fore I was born, so I never saw him.

speaker: ROB
words: This seamstress that you knew in Moscow, when was it that you were working

speaker: ROB
words: with here? About when would that be? You were married in 1913 you say?

speaker: DJB
words: Uh-hm.

speaker: ROB
words: So it would be 1908, 1909 somewhere around there?

speaker: DJB
words: Somewhere long there.

speaker: ROB
words: Around that time, I don't recall if it was a seamstress or a hatmaker, but

speaker: ROB
words: she went up and homesteaded up out north and east of the Bovill country.

speaker: ROB
words: Would that happen to be the same woman? There was quite a long story written

speaker: ROB
words: about her.

speaker: DJB
words: I wouldn't be the same woman because this dressmaker, she had a little

speaker: DJB
words: notion shop too in connection with here. So she was in Moscow for years.

speaker: DJB
words: I can't think what her name was now. She was an old maid, she never had

speaker: DJB
words: married. And afterwards she came down here to Clarkston. Bought a little

speaker: DJB
words: place down there and then she went to northern Californina near Mt. Shasta,

speaker: DJB
words: somewhere in there. I wish I could think of her name.

speaker: ROB
words: It's not particularly important. Was Moscow a pretty peaceful little town

speaker: ROB
words: in those days?

speaker: DJB
words: Oh, it wasn't too bad.

speaker: ROB
words: It was the kind of place that you could feel pretty safe walking around..,

speaker: DJB
words: Oh yeh, I think so.

speaker: ROB
words: ...and not be bothered.

speaker: DJB
words: I think it was awhole lot safer than it is now. (Laughs) Most -any of

speaker: DJB
words: these towns nowadays, I don't know if you're safe in them or not.

speaker: ROB
words: I know during Prohibition some of the smaller towns around the county got

speaker: ROB
words: to be fairly rowdy. Did Moscow stay pretty quiet or did it also...

speaker: DJB
words: Oh, I don't think it was too bad. I don't remember that it was unusual.
